rules.title: Repo descriptor present and valid
rules.lesson: C39 — the repo declares itself; applicability, scrutiny, and ownership consume a schema-validated descriptor, not per-rule tree-sniffing that misfires and can't express intent
rules.rationale: type/lifecycle/maturity/workflow cannot be reliably inferred from the tree. A minimal baseline.repo.json is the one stored intent every derivation hangs off. Absent, the checker falls back to fragile auto-detection; present-but-invalid, its declared intent can't be trusted. Transitional WARN — a repo can adopt incrementally.
rules.fix: Add baseline.repo.json at the repo root: copy templates/baseline.repo.json or a config-presets/*.repo.json posture preset, then set type, lifecycle, maturity, workflow, and anchoring.

rules.title: A descriptor change carries its judgment in the same range
rules.lesson: CF4/FS1 — the descriptor is V2's largest stored intent and a PR must not weaken the posture that judges it; the judgment saying WHY belongs in the same range, not a follow-up promise
rules.rationale: Deterministic, so blocker is lawful: baseline.repo.json appears in the admitted range's diff AND no schema-valid, unexpired judgment whose subject is exactly 'baseline.repo.json' (ONE spelling — the filename, the one constant the tool owns) was added in the same range. The weakening classification (schema x-strictness ladders + gate-consumed set-rules, src/derive/posture.mjs) rides the finding text — M7's per-axis policy seam, not this verdict's fork. The run's own posture comes from the TARGET ref (FS1), so the judged-by descriptor is never the branch's.
rules.fix: In the SAME PR as the descriptor change: baseline jdg new --kind deviation --subject "baseline.repo.json" --reason "why the posture changed" --review-by <date>.
